five-ideas.ideas_ranked:
# Five Product Ideas — Cut the Cord × Field Engineer Brief

---

## #1 — FieldOracle Offline
- **One-line pitch**: On-device RAG assistant that answers technical-manual questions and guides incident protocols with zero connectivity.
- **Target user + moment**: Field engineer on a wind turbine nacelle at 80m altitude, 4G dead, fault code E-402 on the controller — needs the right torque spec and shutdown sequence in <10s.
- **Why cloud fails**: (1) bandwidth — no LTE in remote sites; (2) latency — a 30s round-trip when hands are on a breaker is unsafe; (3) sovereignty — manuals are often NDA'd OEM IP that cannot leave the device.
- **On-device stack**: Ollama runtime · qwen2.5-coder:7b for reasoning + gemma3:4b fallback for low-RAM · SQLite + sqlite-vss for embeddings · all-MiniLM-L6-v2 via llama.cpp for embedding · manuals pre-indexed as PDF→chunks at provision time.
- **60s demo**: Judge pulls Ethernet cable → `netproof.sh` overlay shows 0 egress packets → engineer asks "turbine E-402, safe shutdown steps?" → cited answer in <4s with page refs → judge watches incident log auto-queue while offline → reconnect cable → sync flushes to cloud.
- **Primary benchmark**: Answer accuracy@top-1 on a 50-question manual Q&A set (target: beat naive local baseline by ≥20pp while staying <5s P95 latency on M3 Pro).
- **Feasibility in 24h / 4 people**: **HIGH** — RAG over SQLite is a solved pattern, pipelines already scaffolded, all 5 models pre-pulled. Biggest risk: sourcing a realistic manual corpus + gold Q&A set in time (mitigation: use public Siemens/ABB PDFs).
- **Moat**: Tight eval harness (benchmarks/ already designed) + citation-grounded answers with page-level provenance + the zero-egress tcpdump proof in `evidence/` — reproducing all three in a weekend is the barrier, not the RAG itself.

---

## #2 — BlackBox Incident Logger
- **One-line pitch**: Voice-first offline incident capture that structures free-form dictation into a compliant JSON report and syncs on reconnect.
- **Target user + moment**: Offshore rig technician post-gas-leak alarm, hands greasy, gloves on, needs to log timestamp/location/parts-affected before memory fades — 40 min from shore Wi-Fi.
- **Why cloud fails**: (1) privacy — incident reports contain personnel names + safety-critical data that must not touch third-party clouds pre-legal-review; (2) bandwidth — offshore sat links are metered; (3) latency — streaming STT to cloud fails at 50kbps.
- **On-device stack**: whisper.cpp (tiny.en or base.en) for STT · phi4-mini for structuring → JSON schema · SQLite WAL for durable offline queue · background sync daemon with exponential backoff.
- **60s demo**: Judge speaks "Valve V-23 leaking, 0900 UTC, shut off B-line, tagged out" → whisper transcribes in real-time → phi4-mini emits strict-schema JSON on screen → airplane-mode toggle → 3 more incidents queued → disable airplane mode → sync bar flushes to mock endpoint.
- **Primary benchmark**: Structured-extraction F1 on a 30-incident gold set vs. cloud GPT-4o-mini baseline (target: ≥0.85 F1 fully offline).
- **Feasibility**: **HIGH** — phi4-mini already pulled, whisper.cpp has Mac binaries, SQLite-queue pattern is trivial. Biggest risk: whisper STT accuracy with industrial background noise (mitigation: demo in quiet room, document noise limitation honestly).
- **Moat**: The JSON schema + eval harness on structured extraction is where the signal lives; anyone can bolt whisper to an LLM, but proving 0.85 F1 offline in 24h requires the benchmark infrastructure we've already spec'd.

---

## #3 — SyncVault Field Kit (combined)
- **One-line pitch**: One app that fuses offline manual Q&A + voice incident logging + CRDT-based sync, so the engineer has a single pane on the truck tablet.
- **Target user + moment**: Rural fiber-splicing technician at a street cabinet — needs both the splice-loss troubleshooting flow AND to log the ticket resolution, with sync to dispatch when they drive back into 4G.
- **Why cloud fails**: Combines #1 + #2 reasons (bandwidth + privacy + sovereignty) plus the real win: **operational continuity** — a fragmented offline/online experience kills adoption.
- **On-device stack**: Ollama (qwen2.5-coder:7b) · whisper.cpp · SQLite + sqlite-vss · Automerge (CRDT) for multi-device conflict-free sync · Tauri shell for cross-platform desktop.
- **60s demo**: Split-screen: Q&A pane answers "splice-loss >0.3 dB, what's the SOP?" while voice pane captures the ticket resolution → judge unplugs network → both keep working → reconnect → CRDT sync merges with a second laptop that was also editing offline, no conflicts.
- **Primary benchmark**: End-to-end task-completion time (Q&A + log + sync) vs. cloud-only baseline under simulated 0-kbps/intermittent-link conditions.
- **Feasibility**: **MEDIUM** — combines two stacks + CRDT layer + UI polish in 24h is ambitious. Biggest risk: UI/integration time eating the benchmark budget (mitigation: pre-commit to a Tauri template, keep UI utilitarian).
- **Moat**: CRDT multi-device demo is the wow-factor judges rarely see; showing truly conflict-free offline collaboration on two laptops is a memorable visual competitors can't fake.

---

## #4 — SOP Copilot Walkie
- **One-line pitch**: Hands-free walkie-talkie-style voice assistant that reads incident protocols step-by-step, triggered by push-to-talk.
- **Target user + moment**: Electrical lineman up a pole with both hands on a bucket — can't look at a screen, needs the next lockout-tagout step read aloud.
- **Why cloud fails**: (1) safety-critical latency — a 3s cloud hop on "confirm breaker open?" is dangerous mid-procedure; (2) no LTE on rural utility poles; (3) hands-free requires sub-second wake-word that cloud TTS can't guarantee.
- **On-device stack**: Porcupine/openWakeWord for wake detection · whisper.cpp base.en · gemma3:4b (small, fast) for protocol-tree traversal · Piper TTS for voice output · hardcoded JSON protocol trees (not RAG — determinism matters for safety).
- **60s demo**: Judge says "Hey Oracle, start lockout tagout" → Piper reads step 1 → judge says "done" → step 2 → airplane mode toggled mid-procedure → no degradation → full 5-step SOP completed hands-free.
- **Primary benchmark**: Wake-to-first-audio latency P95 (target: <800ms on M3 Pro) + SOP step-accuracy (100% on known protocols, since deterministic).
- **Feasibility**: **MEDIUM** — TTS + wake-word + STT + LLM orchestration has many moving parts, audio on macOS is fiddly. Biggest risk: Piper TTS voice quality sounding demo-grade (mitigation: have a pre-recorded fallback voice).
- **Moat**: Full voice loop staying sub-second end-to-end offline is hard to replicate; most hackathon teams will skip TTS entirely. But the determinism (no RAG) reduces the AI moat somewhat.

---

## #5 — RedactRelay Field Sync
- **One-line pitch**: Offline assistant that auto-redacts PII/customer-sensitive data from logs locally before any eventual cloud sync.
- **Target user + moment**: Medical-device field engineer servicing an MRI in a hospital basement — logs may contain patient room numbers, must be stripped before GDPR-compliant sync.
- **Why cloud fails**: (1) sovereignty — GDPR Art. 9 health-adjacent data cannot leave EU/device without processing; (2) privacy — the redaction itself is the point, defeating the purpose of cloud-side redaction.
- **On-device stack**: Ollama (qwen2.5-coder:3b) for NER + redaction decisions · Presidio-style rule engine as belt-and-braces · SQLite with redacted + original tables · selective-sync daemon (redacted only).
- **60s demo**: Engineer types "Serviced MRI in Room 412 for Mrs. Rossi today" → on-screen preview shows redacted version "Serviced MRI in [LOC] for [PERSON] today" before sync → airplane-mode toggle → log persists → reconnect → only redacted version leaves the device (tcpdump proves it).
- **Primary benchmark**: PII recall/precision on a synthetic field-log dataset vs. cloud-based Presidio baseline (target: ≥0.9 recall).
- **Feasibility**: **MEDIUM-LOW** — redaction eval datasets are specialized, building a credible gold set in 24h is hard. Biggest risk: judges asking "why not just regex?" — the LLM-value-add story must be airtight (mitigation: show ambiguous cases regex misses, like nicknames or partial addresses).
- **Moat**: The dual-table architecture + network-level proof-of-redaction is technically real but narrower than the other ideas; a determined competitor could clone the regex baseline and claim parity.

---

## Ranking — Best Fit to Worst Fit

1. **FieldOracle Offline** — Best fit: directly answers the brief's "immediate access to technical manuals and incident protocols" requirement, highest feasibility, and maps 1:1 onto the existing RAG + benchmark infrastructure already designed in `benchmarks/` and `doc/specs/cut-the-cord/BENCHMARKS.md`.
2. **BlackBox Incident Logger** — Strong fit on the "log incidents offline with automatic sync" half of the brief, but narrower than #1 because it skips the manual-retrieval leg; still high-feasibility and a clean structured-extraction benchmark.
3. **SyncVault Field Kit** — The most ambitious and complete answer to the full brief (manuals + logging + sync), but the 24h/4-person feasibility tax from combining three stacks + CRDT + UI drops it below the two focused plays.
4. **SOP Copilot Walkie** — Novel hands-free angle that would wow judges, but deterministic protocol trees reduce the "AI" story and audio plumbing on macOS eats surprising amounts of time; high demo risk.
5. **RedactRelay Field Sync** — Technically sound privacy story but solves the narrowest slice of the brief (sync hygiene, not the engineer's primary task), and the regex-vs-LLM defense is shakier under judge scrutiny than the other four.

crew-critique.critique_and_verdict:
**PRODUCT reviewer** — The brief is explicit: field engineers need (a) manual lookup and (b) incident logging, offline, with sync. Idea #1 (FieldOracle Offline) lands the cleanest narrative punch because judges will viscerally feel the pain in the 60s demo — the Ethernet-unplug moment plus a cited answer with page refs is the kind of shot that gets screenshotted. #2 is a solid B-plot but only covers half the brief and lacks the 'aha' visual — watching JSON populate is less cinematic than watching a technician get a life-safety answer with no network. #3 is the most complete answer on paper and the CRDT two-laptop sync is genuinely memorable, but a 24h hackathon demo that tries to tell three stories usually tells none of them well; judges reward one crisp narrative over a fragmented tour. #4 is charming but 'walkie-talkie' framing drifts from the brief's manual/protocol focus and deterministic trees undercut the AI story judges came to see. #5 solves a real GDPR pain but it's the supporting actor, not the hero — engineers don't wake up thinking 'I need redaction today,' they think 'I need the answer.' Pick #1 and make the zero-egress tcpdump overlay the emotional centerpiece.

**TECHNICAL reviewer** — On M3 Pro + MSI Stealth in 24h with 4 people, the feasibility gradient is stark. #1 rides on a solved pattern (Ollama + SQLite + sqlite-vss + MiniLM embeddings), all models are pre-pulled per the playbook, and the benchmark harness in `benchmarks/` is already scaffolded — the critical-path work collapses to corpus ingestion, a 50-question gold set, and eval runs. qwen2.5-coder:7b at ~5GB fits comfortably in M3 Pro unified memory with headroom for embedding model + OS, and <5s P95 on short-context RAG is realistic (expect 1.5–3s with a tight retrieval window). #2 is also feasible but whisper.cpp base.en plus phi4-mini plus a strict JSON schema adds a structured-output reliability dimension that eats debugging time. #3 is the trap: Automerge + Tauri + two stacks + UI polish in 24h is where teams die at 4am debugging IPC. #4 has audio-device plumbing on macOS (CoreAudio + wake-word thresholds + Piper pipeline latency) that routinely consumes half a sprint; sub-800ms P95 wake-to-audio is achievable but fragile on demo night. #5 is medium-feasible but gold-dataset construction in 24h is the bottleneck, not the model. Pick #1 — the work-to-evidence ratio is unbeatable.

**SKEPTIC reviewer** — Let me poke holes. #1's honest risk is 'is this just LlamaIndex with extra steps?' — the moat claim rests entirely on the eval harness, the citation provenance, and the `netproof.sh` tcpdump proof; if any of those three are half-baked on demo day, it collapses into 'RAG wrapper.' That said, the zero-egress packet capture is a real, reproducible artifact competitors won't bother to build, and page-level citations are a verifiable non-wrapper signal. #2 has a benchmark trap: comparing offline phi4-mini structured extraction against GPT-4o-mini F1 is a losing framing — even 0.85 F1 sounds weak next to GPT-4o's ~0.95; reframe as 'offline parity within 10pp at zero bandwidth cost' or don't show the number. #3's CRDT demo is the kind of thing that works in rehearsal and flakes live; also Tauri + Automerge is a hidden dependency chain that could smuggle in Electron-era bloat. #4's hidden cloud risk is zero (Piper + whisper.cpp + Porcupine are all genuinely local), but 'deterministic protocol tree' means the LLM is doing almost no work — a judge asking 'what's the AI doing here?' would be awkward. #5's biggest trap is the regex baseline: if the gold set isn't adversarial enough to expose regex failures, the LLM's added value looks cosmetic. #1 has the fewest cloud-dependency skeletons and the most falsifiable claims — pick it, but make the proof artifacts bulletproof.

**SYNTHESIS VERDICT** — Execute **#1 FieldOracle Offline**. It wins on all three axes: the cleanest narrative for judges, the highest feasibility on the existing repo scaffolding, and the most defensible 'not-a-wrapper' story via citations + netproof + eval harness. Top 3 risks: (1) **corpus + gold-set quality** — a weak 50-question set undermines the benchmark, so lock this down in the first 4 hours using public Siemens/ABB PDFs and have one person own curation end-to-end; (2) **citation fidelity** — page-reference grounding must actually work, not hallucinate page numbers, because a single wrong cite on demo day destroys credibility; (3) **demo-night fragility of the zero-egress proof** — `netproof.sh` must be rehearsed on the exact demo machine with the exact network config, because a tcpdump surprise on stage is unrecoverable. Top 3 accelerators from the pre-built repo: (1) **`benchmarks/` harness + `doc/specs/cut-the-cord/BENCHMARKS.md`** — eval infrastructure is already designed, just plug in the Q&A set; (2) **pre-pulled models (qwen2.5-coder:7b, gemma3:4b, all-MiniLM-L6-v2)** and the ClaudeFlow pipelines under `pipelines/cut-the-cord/` mean no cold-start download or orchestration work; (3) **`scripts/netproof.sh` + `scripts/doctor.sh` + the playbook at `doc/specs/cut-the-cord/00-TEAM_PLAYBOOK.md`** — the evidence-capture and environment-sanity tooling is already wired, so the team can focus on the corpus, the UI shell, and the demo choreography rather than plumbing.

demo-plan.execution_plan:
## Chosen idea
- **name**: FieldOracle Offline
- **one-line pitch**: An air-gapped field-engineer copilot that answers protocol/manual questions with page-level citations on an unplugged MacBook, with a live tcpdump overlay proving zero network egress.

## Target user + failure-of-cloud justification
**User**: Industrial field engineers servicing PLCs, drives, and safety controllers at customer sites (Siemens S7, ABB AC500, Rockwell ControlLogix). They arrive at plants where (a) the customer's network policy forbids external connections on the OT segment, (b) cellular is blocked by faraday-cage concrete, or (c) troubleshooting a tripped safety relay is life-critical and cannot wait for a VPN handshake. **Cloud fails here three ways**: compliance (IEC 62443 / customer NDA bans exfiltration of plant topology embedded in the question), latency (RTT over a tethered phone at a substation is 800ms–3s, unusable mid-troubleshoot), and liability (a wrong answer from a cloud model with no audit trail is unshippable — on-device + cited pages means every answer is traceable to a specific manual page).

## Scope (what ships in 24h)
- **In**: (1) RAG pipeline over 3 curated Siemens/ABB/Rockwell PDF manuals (~800 pages total), chunked with page-level metadata; (2) Ollama runtime with qwen2.5-coder:7b (answerer) + all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (embedder) via sqlite-vss; (3) minimal Tauri-or-web UI shell with query box, streaming answer, and citation chips showing `[manual, page N]` that expand to the source passage; (4) 50-question gold eval set with retrieval@5 and answer-grounding scoring, runnable via the existing `benchmarks/` harness; (5) `scripts/netproof.sh` tcpdump overlay rehearsed on the demo MacBook with Ethernet unplugged, captured as both live feed and pre-recorded fallback.
- **Out**: multi-user sync, incident logging/forms, voice input, Windows/MSI Stealth parity build, PII redaction layer, fine-tuning, corpus beyond the 3 anchor manuals, user auth, installer packaging.

## Solution stack (runtime, models, data layer, UI shell)
- **Runtime**: Ollama 0.x local, invoked via HTTP on 127.0.0.1:11434; warmstart via `scripts/warmstart.sh` pre-demo.
- **Models**: `qwen2.5-coder:7b` (Q4_K_M, ~4.7GB) as the generator with 4k context window and tight retrieval (top-5 chunks, ~1.8k tokens); `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (ONNX, 384-dim) as the embedder. Fallback generator: `gemma3:4b` if memory pressure spikes.
- **Data layer**: SQLite with sqlite-vss extension for HNSW vector search; schema `chunks(id, manual_id, page, section, text, embedding)`. Ingestion via a one-shot ClaudeFlow pipeline `pipelines/cut-the-cord/ingest-corpus.yaml` that runs PDF→text (pdfplumber) → semantic chunker (512-token windows, 64-token overlap, page-boundary respected) → embed → insert.
- **UI shell**: Lightweight Vite + React single-page app served locally on :5173, talking to a thin FastAPI wrapper on :8000 that does retrieval + prompt assembly + Ollama streaming. Citation chips are first-class: every claim in the answer is post-processed to attach the supporting chunk's `(manual, page)` tuple; if a claim has no supporting chunk above cosine 0.35, it is stripped and replaced with "Not in corpus."

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] **Zero egress verified**: `scripts/netproof.sh` shows zero outbound packets to non-loopback destinations during a 5-query session on demo machine with Wi-Fi off and Ethernet unplugged.
- [ ] **Citation fidelity**: ≥95% of citations on the 50-question gold set point to a page whose text actually contains the quoted or paraphrased answer span (measured by substring/embedding overlap ≥0.6).
- [ ] **P95 latency <5s**: end-to-end query-to-final-token P95 under 5 seconds on M3 Pro (target 2–3s median) across the 50-question set, measured via `benchmarks/` harness.
- [ ] **Retrieval@5 ≥0.80**: for the 50-question gold set, the correct source page appears in the top-5 retrieved chunks at least 80% of the time.

## 24-hour timeline (T+0 to T+24, gates every 4h)
- **T+0 → T+4 (Setup & corpus lock)**: One owner curates 3 PDFs + drafts 50 Q&A pairs with ground-truth pages. Two owners scaffold FastAPI + ingestion pipeline + sqlite-vss schema. One owner wires `netproof.sh` + `doctor.sh` on the demo MacBook, verifies Ollama warmstart. **Gate: corpus ingested, 10 sanity queries return plausible chunks, netproof produces a clean zero-egress log.**
- **T+4 → T+8 (RAG loop closed)**: Prompt template locked with citation post-processor. First 20 gold questions run end-to-end through the harness. UI shell renders streaming answer + citation chips. **Gate: retrieval@5 ≥0.70 on first 20 Qs; UI shows a cited answer on stage-machine resolution.**
- **T+8 → T+12 (Eval + tune)**: Full 50-question run. Tune chunk size, top-k, cosine threshold for citation stripping. Swap to `gemma3:4b` if latency P95 >5s on qwen. **Gate: retrieval@5 ≥0.80, P95 <5s, citation fidelity ≥0.90.**
- **T+12 → T+16 (Polish + demo prep)**: UI styling pass (citation chips, empty-state, "Not in corpus" badge), 60s demo script drafted, netproof overlay rehearsed 3× end-to-end with Ethernet unplug moment timed. **Gate: one clean run of the full 60s demo recorded as fallback video.**
- **T+16 → T+20 (Hardening)**: Kill edge cases — empty retrieval, model timeout, UI refresh mid-stream. Pre-warm model + pre-load UI. Write one-page README covering how to reproduce the benchmark. **Gate: 3 consecutive clean demo runs with no operator intervention.**
- **T+20 → T+24 (Buffer + rehearsal)**: 4h reserved for the inevitable unknown. Final demo rehearsals on venue Wi-Fi (then unplugged), pitch deck trimmed to 3 slides, gold-set numbers locked into the pitch. **Gate: pitch + demo rehearsed twice on demo hardware, final numbers frozen in a screenshot committed to the repo.**

## Primary benchmark scenario + what "winning" means numerically
**Scenario**: 50-question gold set over 3 industrial manuals, executed on MacBook M3 Pro with Wi-Fi off + Ethernet unplugged, `scripts/netproof.sh` running in parallel. Each question has a ground-truth `(manual, page)` pair and a reference answer span.
**Winning numerically**:
- Retrieval@5 ≥ **0.80** (stretch 0.90)
- Answer-grounding fidelity ≥ **0.95** (cited page actually contains the answer)
- P95 end-to-end latency < **5s** (stretch: median <2.5s)
- Outbound packets to non-loopback destinations during the run: **exactly 0** (tcpdump log committed alongside benchmark CSV)
- Comparison framing in pitch: "on-device parity with GPT-4o-mini on grounding, at zero bandwidth, zero egress, fully auditable citations" — we do NOT show a raw F1 vs GPT-4o number; we show the four metrics above plus the netproof artifact.

## Pitch hook (one sentence, memorable)
"We pulled the Ethernet cable, asked a PLC safety-relay question, and got a cited answer with a page reference in 2.1 seconds — and here's the tcpdump log proving not a single byte left the laptop."

## Demo 60-second sequence (who does what, second-by-second)
- **0:00–0:05** — Presenter (Engineer 1) on stage: "This is FieldOracle. Offline copilot for industrial field engineers. Watch the network indicator." Laptop visible on projector, Wi-Fi icon off, Ethernet cable plugged in.
- **0:05–0:10** — Engineer 2 (at laptop) physically unplugs Ethernet cable on camera. Network indicator goes red. `netproof.sh` overlay shown in bottom-right corner of screen, counter at 0 outbound packets.
- **0:10–0:18** — Presenter types the question into the UI: *"Siemens S7-1500: what's the procedure to reset a tripped F-DI safety input after an external fault?"*
- **0:18–0:22** — Answer streams in. First token at ~0.4s, full answer at ~2.1s. Citation chips `[Siemens S7-1500 Safety Manual, p. 147]` and `[p. 152]` appear inline.
- **0:22–0:30** — Presenter clicks a citation chip. The source passage expands in a side panel, page 147, with the relevant paragraph highlighted. "Every answer is grounded. No hallucinated pages."
- **0:30–0:38** — Presenter points to the netproof overlay: "Zero outbound packets. This is the tcpdump log, committed in the repo next to the benchmark CSV. 50 questions, retrieval@5 of 0.86, P95 latency 3.2 seconds, zero egress."
- **0:38–0:48** — Engineer 2 runs a second query: *"ABB AC500: default baud rate for Modbus RTU on COM1?"* — answer + citation in ~1.8s. "This is the shot a field engineer needs at 2am in a substation with no signal."
- **0:48–0:55** — Final slide: four numbers (retrieval@5, grounding, P95, egress=0) + the phrase "Not a wrapper. A proof."
- **0:55–0:60** — Presenter: "FieldOracle. The only copilot that works when the network doesn't — and proves it."

## Biggest risk + mitigation
**Risk**: Citation fidelity collapses on demo day — the model produces a confident answer with a wrong page reference, and a judge catches it. This single failure destroys the "not-a-wrapper" narrative that the entire idea rests on.
**Mitigation (four layers)**: (1) **Hard citation gate** — the prompt never asks the model to "cite" freely; instead, post-generation, every factual claim is matched against the retrieved chunks via a cosine-similarity check (threshold 0.35), and any claim without a supporting chunk is replaced with "Not in corpus" rather than guessed. (2) **Gold-set coverage on risky categories** — the 50-question set includes 10 deliberately adversarial questions (out-of-corpus, ambiguous pages, near-duplicate sections) so we catch fidelity failures before the demo. (3) **Demo questions are pre-validated** — the 3–4 questions used in the live demo are confirmed to hit retrieval@5=1.0 and grounding=1.0 on the final model config, documented in `doc/specs/cut-the-cord/demo-script.md`. (4) **Fallback video** — a pre-recorded clean run of the 60s demo is loaded on the presenter's laptop; if the live run fails, the presenter switches to the video and narrates over it rather than debugging on stage.

outputSnapshot.pitch_30s: It's 2 a.m. in a concrete substation. A Siemens safety relay just tripped, the line is down, and your phone shows no signal — cellular RTT here is 800 milliseconds to 3 seconds, when it works at all. You need page 147 of the safety manual, now. FieldOracle Offline answers PLC questions with cited manual pages in 2 seconds on an unplugged laptop — and proves zero bytes left the machine.
